title: The Install Base Flywheel: Why Huang Thinks Frontier Labs Are the Wrong Lens for CUDA's Value

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
A persistent critique of Nvidia's CUDA moat is that it matters most to small developers, not to the hyperscalers and frontier labs that drive the majority of Nvidia's revenue. These large customers can write custom kernels, build their own frameworks like Triton, and even compile to non-Nvidia accelerators. Jensen Huang acknowledges all of this—and argues it misses the point entirely. The value of CUDA, he contends, is not that any single customer cannot leave, but that the aggregate ecosystem makes leaving irrational.

Huang frames CUDA's advantage in three layers. First is ecosystem richness: every AI framework, every reinforcement learning toolkit, every inference engine runs on CUDA. When something breaks in a complex training run, developers want to trust the foundation beneath them. "You would like it to always be you and to be able to trust the computer," Huang explains—a reliability argument that becomes more compelling as AI systems grow in complexity. Second is the install base: several hundred million GPUs spanning generations from the P-series through Blackwell, deployed in every cloud and on-premises configuration. Any software written for CUDA runs on the largest possible fleet, which matters enormously for framework builders and AI startups choosing where to invest engineering effort.

The third layer is what Huang calls versatility of deployment. Nvidia GPUs exist in every major cloud—Google, Amazon, Azure, OCI—as well as on-premises. For an AI startup uncertain about which cloud partner to choose, or a robotics company that needs the same stack to run inside a physical robot, CUDA's ubiquity eliminates an entire category of platform risk. This is distinct from raw performance; it's an argument about optionality and reach.

Huang also pushes back on the idea that hyperscaler revenue is primarily internal consumption. He claims most Nvidia GPU usage at AWS, Azure, and OCI serves external customers—the tens of thousands of AI companies building on Nvidia's architecture. The hyperscalers favor Nvidia not just for price-performance but because Nvidia's ecosystem delivers the broadest customer base to fill their GPU capacity. This creates a flywheel: more developers choose CUDA because it's the most abundant architecture, which drives more cloud investment in Nvidia hardware, which makes it even more abundant.

The "F1 racer" analogy captures Huang's view of the optimization gap. While anyone can drive a GPU at baseline performance, Nvidia's engineering teams routinely deliver 2–3x speedups when they optimize a partner's stack—gains that translate directly to revenue at fleet scale. Even in a world where AI can help write kernels, Huang argues that Nvidia's intimate knowledge of its own architecture will remain a persistent advantage, making the CUDA ecosystem less about lock-in and more about the compounding returns of deep co-optimization.
